Please describe your research career to date
Clinical career: This fellowship combines my passion for research and evidence-based practice with my clinical
aims of providing high quality intervention which enables everyday participation, strengthens relationships and
provides holistic, person-centred care. I have eight years’ professional experience as a speech and language
therapist (SLT). During this time, I have developed specialist skills in working with people living with Parkinson’s
and other neurological conditions with associated communication and cognitive difficulties. Over the last two years
I have gained unique clinical experience in delivering the novel therapy approach, Better Conversations with
Parkinson’s (BCP), which this proposal aims to develop.
Research experience: My clinical-academic career began whilst working as a local Research Champion for
Gloucestershire Health and Care NHS Foundation Trust. I successfully supported the Research and Development
team to raise the profile of research and promote recruitment amongst clinical teams through emails, blog posts
and presentations. I also conducted my own qualitative research study, which was the first of its kind to explore the
effects of mask wearing for people with communication difficulties post-stroke. I was awarded a research training
award to develop my design and protocol and conduct the research. My report has since been published in a peer-
reviewed journal, and I presented my findings at an international conference. Feedback indicated that the paper
addressed a ‘highly topical issue globally’, comprised of robust methodology and was of ‘interest to SLTs around
the world’.
These experiences enabled me to secure a job in 2021 as the Research Assistant on a Parkinson’s UK funded
project based at University College London (UCL) investigating the feasibility of Better Conversations with
Parkinson’s via telehealth (BCP-Telehealth) (DOI:10.2196/41416 ). This study is a precursor to the current
proposal. As the sole Research Assistant, I have an in-depth knowledge of this research area, a network of
relationships with relevant stakeholders, and essential research skills directly related to this proposal. I have been
responsible for defining and managing the research project in collaboration with the research team and key
stakeholders, ensuring all relevant ethical and data protection issues are followed, and leading the design, delivery
and evaluation of the feasibility study. I therefore feel confident I can deliver this proposed fellowship’s aims within
the timeline. My experience has enabled me to gain expertise in Patient and Public Involvement (PPI) and develop
skills towards my long-term aim of becoming a leading practitioner-academic.
PPI: I have gained expertise in PPI through facilitating two advisory groups, and co-producing the BCP-Telehealth
therapy programme and website resources (www.betterconversations-parkinsons.com) with key stakeholders
(people living with Parkinson’s, family members, SLTs, researchers). I led an engagement project with people
living with Parkinson’s and SLTs to co-decide the research priorities which directly informed this Doctoral Clinical
and Practitioner Academic Fellowship proposal. These priorities included identifying suitable outcome measures
and preparing for a full-scale BCP trial. I have built strong networks with people living with Parkinson’s,
Parkinson’s UK, SLTs and researchers in order to develop the intervention BCP-Telehealth and disseminate study
results. Members of this engagement network support, and look to be involved in, future BCP research.
Impact: I have led in sharing the progress of BCP-Telehealth research with key stakeholders via social media,
blog posts and presentations, including building @BCDParkinsons’ X (Twitter) profile, with approximately 400
followers. I was lead author on the project protocol, which was published in JMIR Research Protocols [1] to
promote research transparency. I have also presented the project at the SLT Parkinson’s Network conference in
June 2023, and have been invited to present BCP-Telehealth to local research network groups and four NHS
speech and language therapy teams. Three of these NHS speech and language therapy teams have subsequently
chosen to take part in the BCP feasibility study proposed as part of this fellowship.
I have shared leading and novel work on co-production via a case study, under review for publication in the
Bulletin (the Royal College of Speech and Language Therapists’ (RCSLT) members’ magazine), and in
collaboration with the Parkinson’s UK research team. I led the co-writing of an article with PPI group members
about BCP-Telehealth’s co-production process, which is under review for publication in the peer-reviewed journal
Research for All.
Leadership and supervision: This fellowship is designed to further develop my skills in professional leadership
and support my long-term goal of becoming a leading practitioner academic. I have taken opportunities to develop
leadership skills throughout my clinical and research career. I am currently a member of a working party updating
the UK Parkinson’s Excellence Network SLT Parkinson’s Guidance, and have been involved in national initiatives
such as the RCSLT working party on long COVID rehabilitation. In 2022 I completed the Allied Health Professions
Federation Leadership Programme. This has allowed me to demonstrate and develop my own leadership qualities
and gain understanding of the principles of governance, financial management and leadership of an association.
I have also sought out opportunities to develop my supervisory and teaching skills. I have co-supervised Master’s
dissertations, and lectured on the Speech and Language Sciences Masters programme at UCL. These activities
are contributing towards my portfolio application for Associate Fellowship of the Higher Education Academy.
Career development: My achievements in my current role demonstrate my commitment to this research topic,
which I intend to further develop during my PhD. I am actively working with my proposed academic supervisors
and have engaged with five people living with Parkinson’s and twelve SLTs to identify research priorities for this
proposal. I therefore have an established network of experts to support this proposal. Through this fellowship I
plan to develop additional highly specialist clinical skills in the management of people living with Parkinson’s, in

APPLICATION DETAILS.Plain English Summary of Research:
Parkinson’s is a condition affecting around 120,000 people in the UK. Approximately 80% of people living with
Parkinson’s (plwP) face communication challenges. These can cause embarrassment, isolation and depression.
PlwP and their family members therefore describe needing support with conversation.
Speech and language therapy helps with speech difficulties, such as a quiet voice. However, there is limited
research on how to help plwP with other communication difficulties. These include difficulties finding words,
changed facial expressions, and problems with thinking. This is why I am developing a new therapy called Better
Conversations with Parkinson’s (BCP). BCP involves working with plwP and a family member or friend
(communication partner). Together they build skills to make long-term improvements in everyday conversations.
BCP is in the early stages of development. So far, the therapy (BCP-Telehealth) has only been delivered by a
researcher via video appointment, and to plwP with mild symptoms.
I have worked extensively with plwP and speech and language therapists (SLTs). They came up with these
research priorities:
1. Understand what plwP hope to change by completing BCP and how to measure these changes. This is
useful for SLTs and to show whether BCP is making a difference
2. Modify BCP-Telehealth so that SLTs can use it with people with more advanced Parkinson’s. This includes
making it a face-to-face therapy
3. Find out if changes are needed to the modified version of BCP and the research process. This is important
to prepare for a larger trial looking at effectiveness
This PhD study will address these aims with three work packages.
Work package 1: I will review the literature to come up with a list of all the existing ways of measuring changes in
communication and its impact (for example on relationships and confidence). This will include outcome measures
for plwP and their communication partners. I will ask plwP, communication partners and SLTs to agree what is
most important to them to achieve from BCP therapy. A Patient and Public Involvement (PPI) group will match
these priorities to outcome measures from the literature. The PPI group will include plwP, family members and
SLTs. They will choose outcome measures to test in a trial (work package 3).
Work package 2: I will work with PPI members (plwP, family members and expert SLTs) in a ‘co-production’ group.
This means everyone’s views will be treated equally. We will update BCP so that it can be delivered face-to-face
and used with people with a range of difficulties with communication and thinking. It will also reflect the key areas
of change identified as important in work package 1.
Work package 3: SLTs at four NHS sites will recruit 40 plwP and communication partners. Participants will be
chosen at random to receive the updated BCP therapy or standard speech and language therapy. Participants will
complete outcome measures from work package 1. Results of the trial will be analysed using questionnaires, video
recordings of participants, interviews, and data about how many people are recruited and stay in the trial.
BCP aims to improve the success of conversations between plwP and their communication partners. I hope to
improve confidence in communicating and quality of life, and reduce burden on carers. This could potentially
reduce NHS costs. This study is a step towards a larger trial and economic evaluation to find out whether BCP
does result in these changes. BCP therapy will be shared for free, for SLTs to use with plwP.
I will work with PPI members to share the results of this study through: presentations to Parkinson’s groups,
researchers and professionals; a website and blog; publications; and information for plwP.
APPLICATION DETAILS.Scientific Abstract:
Communication difficulties affect approximately 80% of people living with Parkinson’s (plwP), with significant
effects on quality of life, confidence and relationships. Better Conversations with Parkinson’s (BCP) is being
developed for SLTs to use to meet the needs of plwP and their communication partners (family members/ friends),
who describe wanting to improve day-to-day conversations.
BCP involves the SLT coaching plwP and a communication partner to build long-term skills for more successful
conversations. The approach currently exists as BCP-Telehealth, delivered by a researcher via video appointment.
This proposal uses the Medical Research Council’s framework, refining the intervention and resolving
uncertainties as a step towards a future effectiveness study. Patient and public involvement (PPI) group members
have identified these aims as priorities for research development:
1). To develop an updated set of outcome measures for SLTs to use with BCP and for a future trial
2). To refine BCP intervention so it is: acceptable to plwP with a range of communication impairments, and their
communication partners; and addresses more advanced symptoms including cognitive change. This includes
adapting BCP-Telehealth for face-to-face delivery
3). To establish the feasibility of a multi-centre, randomised controlled trial of BCP
These aims will be achieved through three work packages.
Work package 1 (years 1-2):
A scoping review of the literature will be used to identify existing outcome measures related to communication
changes and their impact on plwP and communication partners. Consensus methods will be used with plwP,
communication partners and expert SLTs to agree on constructs that are most important to achieve when
undergoing BCP therapy. The study PPI group will match these constructs to existing outcome measures identified
in the scoping review and select outcome measures for a feasibility study (work package 3).
Work package 2 (years 2-3):
A PPI network including plwP, family members and expert SLTs will co-produce a refined BCP intervention, so that
it is acceptable for plwP who experience more complex communication and cognitive symptoms, therefore
reflecting SLTs’ clinical needs and the needs of plwP. PPI members will be purposively recruited to ensure
inclusion of under-served groups.
Work package 3 (years 3-4):
40 plwP and a communication partner will be randomly allocated to receive BCP or standard NHS speech and
language therapy (defined by a group of expert SLTs) in a feasibility study across four NHS Trusts. Descriptive
statistics and reflexive thematic analysis will be used to analyse trial, assessment and interview data. The aim is to
examine the acceptability of the refined BCP intervention, identify outstanding areas of development and test
research methods and procedures prior to a future full-scale trial.
The research will be disseminated via conferences, publications and social media to academic and professional
audiences, plwP and family members or friends. A website will host resources and information, with the aim of
BCP and corresponding outcome measures being freely available for SLTs to use with plwP in the future. Future
research will evaluate the impact of BCP on communication, quality of life, participation of plwP, carer burden and
NHS costs.
APPLICATION DETAILS.Detailed Research Plan:
The problem:
Parkinson’s is one of the fastest growing neurological conditions in the world, with a recorded UK prevalence of
around 22:10,000 women and 32:10,000 men (2). Approximately 70-90% of plwP experience communication
changes (3). In addition to changes to speech and voice, Parkinson’s can cause a range of communication and
cognitive difficulties, such as difficulties with pragmatics, verbal fluency and processing (4). These challenges can
affect participation in day-to-day life (5), self-esteem and quality of life (6). Furthermore, communication difficulties
can have a devastating impact on family members, relationships and care burden (7), in particular as cognitive
impairments increase (8). These issues contribute significantly to the high societal costs of Parkinson’s (9).
There is currently a lack of evidenced speech and language therapy approaches addressing the wide range of
communication and cognitive difficulties, and psychosocial impacts related to Parkinson’s. Intervention for plwP
predominantly focuses on speech impairments (10). Lee Silverman Voice Treatment (LSVT) (11), a high-intensity
programme that trains people to recalibrate vocal volume, is well evidenced and can result in significant
improvements to vocal loudness and intelligibility. However, research is mostly amongst those with mild to
moderate Parkinson’s (12) and low levels of cognitive impairment (13). This leaves a gap in standard provision for
those with more advanced symptoms. Interventions addressing more complex symptoms are vital given the
progressive nature of Parkinson’s and since a large proportion of plwP are referred to speech and language
therapy in the later stages of the disease (10,14). Speech and language therapy provision can also be limited in its
carryover from therapy sessions to everyday life (10), and not sufficiently include family members in therapy (15).
There is a need for an intervention for plwP and their families that: addresses the range of communication and
cognitive impairments associated with Parkinson’s; considers the impact of these communication difficulties on
everyday conversations; and involves family members or friends.
Research importance:
The speech and language therapy intervention Better Conversations with Parkinson’s (BCP) is designed to
address the range and complexity of communication needs of plwP and their communication partners (family
members or friends). A speech and language therapist (SLT) works with the person with Parkinson’s and a
communication partner to set individualised goals and build skills to improve the success of everyday
conversations. This research involves refining BCP for use with those with more complex cognitive and
communication difficulties, and preparation for a full-scale trial.
Importance for plwP: Through improved everyday conversations, BCP has the potential to improve quality of life,
communicative participation, and feelings of competence and self-esteem for plwP. Improved communication can
influence relationships and reduce isolation, with a possible impact on mental health issues that are prevalent in
Parkinson’s. The refinements proposed to BCP intend to benefit in particular an under-served group in research:
those with advanced Parkinson’s and complex communication or cognitive needs.
Importance for families: By providing therapy to communication partners alongside plwP, BCP aims to reduce
the risk of carer burden and relationship dissatisfaction (7,16) associated with communication difficulties. The
proposed refinements to BCP target carers of people with Parkinson’s and cognitive impairments, who experience
fewer positive interactions and higher carer burden (8). By supporting carers’ communication and interaction, BCP
aligns with priorities in the NHS Long Term plan and NICE guidance (17).
Importance to stakeholders: This proposal is based on research aims co-decided by key stakeholders (plwP,
family members and SLTs). Stakeholder involvement (figure 1) is central to the proposed research which uses co-
production, a method which is recognised as essential when designing complex health and social care
interventions (18). Co-production is consistent with the principles of person-centred and personalised care upheld
within the NHS Long Term Plan. The long-term aim is to produce a freely available, evidence-based intervention
for SLTs to use with plwP and family members or friends, which is acceptable to plwP including those with more
complex and advanced symptoms.
NIHR aims: This project aligns with NIHR’s aims of bringing clinical research to under-served regions and
communities with major health needs, and of improving the lives of people with multiple long-term conditions. The
refinement of BCP is designed to meet the health needs of an under-served group: plwP with more advanced
communication and cognitive symptoms, including dementias. Collaborating NHS Trusts have been chosen with
the aim of increasing recruitment from ethnic minorities and other under-served groups. Jewish and Asian
communities, and plwP with cognitive impairments will be purposively involved in the PPI network to maximise
inclusion across the research project. BCP is a holistic, individualised approach and the feasibility study inclusion
criteria are broad to align with clinical reality and support the inclusion of plwP with other long-term conditions.
Reduced NHS costs: Parkinson’s is a high-cost, high-need condition for the NHS (19), with communication
difficulties related to higher costs (20). By working towards implementation of BCP in the NHS, this research aims
to address costs associated with the impact of Parkinson’s on communication, participation and feelings of
confidence (9). Through optimising communication with carers, there is the potential to reduce resource utilisation
costs, such as hospitalisations, associated with caregiver strain in Parkinson’s (21). The intervention comprises six
sessions in recognition of the fact that many NHS Trusts are unable to routinely offer more intensive therapies (14)
and to ensure no additional costs or time expectations beyond standard care for people with communication
difficulties resulting from Parkinson’s. According to national data on SLT service delivery (22), this allows a £700
saving per person per year in comparison with more intensive therapies such as LSVT.
Review of existing evidence
Communication difficulties in Parkinson’s
Changes to speech, such as voice and articulatory impairments, are well attested and affect over 70% of plwP
(23). Parkinson’s can also affect communication in other ways, for example the production and processing of
grammar, verbal fluency, information content (4), and pragmatic and non-verbal communication (24). These
changes can cause embarrassment and reduced communicative participation (5), and affect feelings of
competence and self-esteem (6).
Standard speech and language therapy for plwP predominantly focuses on speech and impairments (10), and is
effective in the treatment of hypokinetic dysarthria (a motor speech disorder) (25). Well-evidenced treatments such
as LSVT (11) are predominantly researched amongst those with mild to moderate Parkinson’s (12) and suitable for
those who have sufficient cognitive ability, motivation and stimulable voice quality (13). Standard provision
therefore fails to address the needs of those who experience more severe symptoms or communication
impairments beyond speech, in particular cognitive change. SLTs report feeling able to influence voice loudness
and intelligibility, but lacking assessments and interventions to address broader language and cognitive difficulties
(10).
PlwP describe further limitations to speech and language therapy provision such as: difficulty transferring gains to
day-to-day conversations; difficulty continuing exercises at home; and that therapy does not always address
communication problems of concern (26). Suggestions for improvement include increased focus on the
psychosocial aspects of communication, and inclusion of family members in therapy. These perspectives are
shared by SLTs who report feeling less able to address issues of carryover outside of therapy (10) and describe
lacking necessary tools to target interaction or conversation with this client group (27).
Reduced effectiveness of communication can cause frustration and dissatisfaction in both plwP and their
communication partners (7,16). Communication challenges can directly impact on relationships, cause grief and
changes in life roles, all potentially contributing to carer burden and third party disability (7). In particular spouses
of plwP who have cognitive impairments and dementia describe higher levels of relationship dissatisfaction, care
burden and a reduced number of positive interactions with their partners (8). The unique needs of family members
resulting from the communication and cognitive changes in plwP are currently rarely addressed in speech and
language therapy, and family members describe the need for more support to address the broader impact of the
communication disorder (15).
Communication Partner Training and the Better Conversations approach
Interventions are needed that involve both plwP and their communication partners to improve communication
(6,28). This research addresses this need by refining a Communication Partner Training (CPT) intervention called
Better Conversations with Parkinson’s (BCP). CPT is an umbrella term for a speech and language therapy
approach that is effective for a range of communication difficulties (29–31). CPT can enhance functional
communication and participation for people with communication difficulties (29,32) and improve the knowledge,
communication skills, and attitudes of communication partners (29,33,34). Better Conversations is a CPT
approach that involves working with the person with the communication difficulty and a communication partner to
build conversation skills, for more successful everyday interactions (35). The Better Conversations approach is
underpinned by theories of behaviour change, targeting techniques from the Behaviour Change Techniques
Taxonomy (36) to maximise opportunities for positive changes in conversational behaviours. Existing
interventions, such as Better Conversations with Aphasia (37) and Better Conversations with Primary Progressive
Aphasia (31), have shown success in reducing barriers to conversation and can improve use of self-identified
facilitatory strategies (38,39). The Better Conversations approach has been used successfully with a progressive
neurological condition (primary progressive aphasia) and has shown signs of promise when used with plwP in a
proof of concept case study (40).
BCP is in the early stages of research and currently exists as BCP-Telehealth. The acceptability and feasibility of
BCP-Telehealth delivered by a researcher to patients from one NHS setting is being investigated in a feasibility
study (1). BCP-Telehealth comprises six hour-long sessions delivered via video appointment. An SLT uses video
feedback of the person with Parkinson’s and communication partner’s conversations, and coaches them to identify
what works well and the challenges in their conversations, and identify individualised goals. Personalised
strategies to help conversations are practised in sessions and at home, with access to standardised information,
videos and handouts on a website, and email support from the SLT between sessions. The intervention considers
generalisation of skills from conversations as a pair to conversations with others and in the future to improve
everyday conversations. In line with the UK Medical Research Council’s framework for developing and evaluating
complex interventions (18), this research proposes to refine and optimise BCP-Telehealth to maximise the impact
of BCP, and answer outstanding questions as a step towards a future effectiveness and health economic
evaluation study.
Aims
This research will produce a freely available, evidence-based intervention (a refined BCP) for SLTs to use with
plwP and their communication partners, which is acceptable to plwP including those with more complex and
advanced symptoms. BCP is designed to address communication changes related to Parkinson’s, and the impact
on self-esteem, quality of life, participation, caregiver burden and health and social care costs. The long-term aim
will be a large-scale effectiveness trial of BCP for which separate funding will be sought.
I led a PPI project using consensus methods with plwP, family members and expert NHS SLTs to co-identify
outstanding areas of development for BCP (see project plan) and co-decide BCP research priorities. Based on
these priorities, the aims of the fellowship are:
1). To develop a set of outcome measures to use with BCP and for a future trial
2). To refine BCP intervention so it is acceptable to plwP with a range of communication and cognitive
impairments, and their communication partners
3). To establish the feasibility of a multi-centre, randomised controlled trial of BCP
Objectives
These aims will be achieved through 3 work packages (WPs).
WP1: outcome measurement
 Establish what plwP, family members and SLTs hope to achieve by completing BCP therapy
 Identify suitably sensitive and robust therapy outcome measures that measure changes important to plwP,
family members and SLTs, and that align with the expected impacts of BCP therapy
WP2: intervention refinement
 Work with PPI members (plwP, family members, expert SLTs) to refine BCP to meet the needs of plwP
with a range of communication and cognitive symptoms and severities, including adapting BCP-Telehealth
for face-to-face delivery
WP3: randomised feasibility study
Trial the refined BCP intervention with a control group (standard NHS treatment) over 4 NHS sites to ascertain for
a future full trial:
 the acceptability and adherence to an adapted BCP intervention for plwP with a range of cognitive and
communication symptoms and their family members
 a meaningful and sensitive primary outcome measure (from a set of measures decided upon in WP1) –
this will inform a sample size calculation for a future full trial
recruitment and retention rates

 the feasibility of data collection across multiple NHS sites
 costs to inform a future economic evaluation
 whether a randomised controlled trial can be delivered as intended over multiple NHS sites
Project Plan
The WPs follow the ADAPT guidance (41) for adapting interventions to new contexts, and guidance for developing
and evaluating complex interventions (18) as a step towards implementation of BCP at scale.
WP1: Identifying key constructs plwP and SLTs want to change by completing BCP, and corresponding outcome
measures
PPI priorities: PlwP, family members and SLTs want to be able to capture meaningful changes resulting from
BCP. PPI members feel that current BCP-Telehealth outcome measures do not fully capture the potential impact
of the therapy. Outcome measures are felt to be important for clinical use alongside the intervention, and for a
future effectiveness trial.
Scoping review: I will conduct a scoping review to generate a list of relevant outcome measures reported in
previous studies. Whilst there is research into measures used clinically (27), there is no comprehensive review
identifying outcome measures for plwP which considers changes to communication impairment and the impact on
wider domains such as participation and interaction, from the perspective of plwP or communication partners. A
scoping review has been chosen to allow identification of a broad range of outcome measures, accounting for a
diversity of relevant literature and methodologies.
Nominal group technique: Plwp, communication partners and SLTs will be recruited to three groups of 5-8
people. An open call to SLTs will be made via Clinical Excellence Networks, social media and professional
contacts. Purposive sampling will be used to include SLTs with experience of working with plwP, or experience of
CPT to learn from other fields such as aphasia. PlwP and communication partners will be recruited through
Parkinson’s charities, such as Parkinson’s UK, and through social media and flyers at support groups. For
pragmatic reasons participants will need sufficient cognition, communication and mobility to take part in face-to-
face group discussions. SLTs and communication partners will be asked to consider their own perspectives and
concepts of relevance to those with more severe symptoms unable to take part in this study. Purposive sampling
to ensure a range of communication difficulties and severities will be used in an attempt to capture a broad,
although not complete, range of perspectives. Groups will meet face to face two to three times. Using nominal
group technique, consensus will be reached on constructs that are most important to achieve when undergoing
BCP therapy. The study PPI group will match these constructs to existing outcome measures from the scoping
review, and identify any gaps in current outcome measures. The PPI group will select outcome measures to be
trialled in a feasibility study (WP3) and identify which constructs important to plwP and SLTs should be
incorporated into a refined BCP intervention (WP2).
WP2: Co-production of a refined BCP intervention, adapted for people with cognitive difficulties
PPI priorities: Expert SLTs in the NHS recommend adapting BCP-Telehealth for face-to-face delivery and to
ensure that it meets the needs of those with more advanced Parkinson’s. This is required to respond to the need
for evidence-based interventions for plwP with complex cognitive and communication difficulties, and to reflect the
populations SLTs work with. The co-production methods used below respond directly to areas for improvement
identified in a detailed evaluation of BCP PPI methods used to date.
Co-production: PPI members (plwP, family members, expert SLTs) will co-produce an updated intervention and
resources. Co-production will be informed by members’ expertise, what key stakeholders want to achieve from
BCP (WP1) and data from the BCP-Telehealth feasibility study. I will create a co-production network, with 10 core
members. A mixture of face-to-face, one-to-one or small group meetings (2-3 people), and larger group and online
meetings will be used, with a flexible approach to ensure accessibility for people with a range of needs. Members
will be trained in co-production methods to ensure shared understanding and purpose, and so that all are
empowered to fully participate. A person with Parkinson’s will be paid to act as joint co-production facilitator to
increase the strength of voice and accessibility for plwP.
Outputs: Outputs are expected to include session plans, handouts and video examples hosted on a website for
SLTs to use for free. Electronic resources will be put together with the support of UCL Educational Media Creative
Services.
Recruitment: People will be recruited from existing BCP PPI members, who have a thorough knowledge of BCP-
Telehealth research and valuable experience of living with Parkinson’s and communication difficulties. New
members will also be recruited to challenge thinking and bring different perspectives. I have significant experience
of supporting complex communication needs, and will selectively recruit plwP with cognitive difficulties from
rehabilitation clinics. I will also build collaborative relationships and recruit from minority ethnic groups to include
people previously less consulted in Parkinson’s research (42,43). For example, I will attend Hema’s café, a
network for plwP in the Asian community, and the Jewish Care Disability Support and Social group, given the
increased incidence of Parkinson’s amongst people of Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry.
WP3: Feasibility study of refined BCP
PPI priorities: PPI members emphasise the importance of a future full-scale trial of BCP to demonstrate
effectiveness. Given different models of care nationwide, stakeholders feel it is important to trial BCP in different
NHS settings.
Feasibility phase of intervention development: This aims to evaluate the research design and BCP itself prior
to a future full-scale trial (18). This multi-centre randomised feasibility study will compare a treatment group
receiving adapted BCP intervention to a control group receiving standard care. Standard care will be co-defined by
SLTs at participating sites, and is expected to include approximately six sessions of impairment-based therapy per
participant. With PPI support, I will train SLTs to deliver BCP. Covariate adaptive randomisation will be used to
minimise imbalance of severity of Parkinson’s symptoms between the BCP and control group to control for the
possible influence of this on trial conclusions, and to allow for continuous enrolment to the study.
Recruitment: Participants will be recruited who have: a primary diagnosis of Parkinson’s with resulting
communication difficulties; an available family member or friend to take part in therapy; and adequate hearing,
vision and cognition to participate in SLT intervention. Given the high incidence of dementia in advanced
Parkinson’s (44), recruitment criteria will not exclude those with cognitive difficulties or concomitant conditions, to
address the needs of those with advanced Parkinson’s and mimic clinical reality for future implementation.
Recruitment will take place across four NHS sites (North Bristol NHS Trust, University Hospitals of Derby and
Burton NHS Foundation Trust, Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust and Bolton NHS Foundation Trust),
with agreement in principle from SLT managers at these Trusts. Collaborating Trusts have been chosen due to
SLTs indicating sufficient resources to recruit participants and, since caseloads are more diverse, to maximise
opportunities for recruitment from under-served populations.
Sample size: This has been dealt with pragmatically based on SLT reports of caseload size, national audit data
on SLT service delivery, and expected recruitment rates based on a recent Better Conversations trial (45) and SLT
trial with plwP (46). I anticipate recruiting 40 participants over an 18-month period. Achieving a sample size of over
30 should more accurately inform a future full trial (47).
Outcomes: It is expected that outcome measures selected in WP1 might include measures of activity and
participation (Dysarthria Impact Profile (48), Communicative Participation Item Bank (49)), quality of life
(Parkinson's Disease Questionnaire (50), Parkinson’s Disease Questionnaire – Carers (51), Adult Carer Quality of
Life Questionnaire (52)), personalised goals (Goal Attainment Scaling in rehabilitation (53)), and conversation
strategy use (Measure of Participation in Conversation & Measure of Skill in Supported Conversation (54), Better
Conversations Rating Scales (55), Better Conversations checklist of barriers and facilitators (56)).
A double baseline will be conducted before intervention to account for regression to the mean, to ensure it is
acceptable and feasible in a future trial. Outcome measures will be completed one-week and three months post
intervention to examine maintenance of changes. Participants will be supported to make video recordings of
everyday conversations before and after intervention, and be interviewed to understand acceptance and impact of
the intervention. Additional demographic information (age, medication, disease duration, gender, educational level,
ethnicity, relationship of communication partner) will be used alongside assessments of speech, language and
cognition to gain a profile of participants.
Analysis: Speech, communication and quality of life assessment data will be analysed using the Statistical
Package for the Social Sciences. This information will be used to determine a suitably sensitive outcome measure
and sample size for a future next stage clinical trial. Descriptive statistics will be used to report on recruitment,
attendance and attrition rates, to plan the required number of sites to meet the target sample size. Assessment
data will also be used to identify the most appropriate health related quality of life measure to analyse cost-utility.
SLTs will record the time spent preparing for and delivering BCP and standard intervention, to estimate cost of
delivery in terms of time for each trial arm. Acceptability will be analysed using descriptive statistics (adherence
data, participant feedback forms) and reflexive thematic analysis (57) (interviews, participant feedback forms). SLT
questionnaires and follow-up interviews will be analysed using reflexive thematic analysis to identify future training
needs, explore feasibility of delivering the intervention, and capture perceived barriers and facilitators for
implementation of BCP.
Dissemination, outputs and intended impact
This research will refine a speech and language therapy intervention (BCP) so that it meets the needs of plwP with
a range of communication and cognitive symptoms and is feasible to deliver in an NHS setting. The study will
identify the most appropriate outcome measures for clinicians and researchers to measure changes resulting from
BCP. It will also provide the basis to develop a future training programme and manual for SLTs to deliver BCP,
and prepare for a full-scale trial.
Information and intervention resources will be hosted on the existing website (www.betterconversations-
parkinsons.com). General information about the project and communication with Parkinson’s will be available to
the public throughout the project. BCP resources will be made freely available for SLTs to use with plwP on
completion of the study.
Dissemination will be guided by the PPI network and include: joint presentations with PPI members at Parkinson’s
groups, academic conferences, professional clinical excellence networks and to SLTs in NHS Trusts; updates via
established BCP social media, website and blog; accessible information leaflets for service users; publications in
academic journals, professional magazines and service user magazines such as The Parkinson.
Focus on dissemination work will take place: once appropriate outcome measures are identified to influence
clinical and research practice; during and after evaluation of PPI work to share examples of supporting people with
communication difficulties to engage meaningfully with PPI; after production of the BCP intervention to engage
with potential future users and respond to feedback; and following analysis of the feasibility trial.
It is anticipated that the refined BCP intervention will be a valuable resource for SLTs to use with plwP, in
particular for those with cognitive difficulties and advanced symptoms. By improving day-to-day conversations,
BCP can likely improve quality of life for plwP, communicative participation, and feelings of competence and self-
esteem. Directly working with family members to address communication challenges may also reduce caregiver
burden and third party disability.
BCP has potential to reduce NHS costs associated with this high-cost, high-need condition, and save money
compared to more intensive therapies such as LSVT. Through improving everyday conversations between plwP
and carers, there is the possibility of reducing money spent on the effect of Parkinson’s on mood, communication
and confidence, and reducing costs associated with caregiver strain. Feasibility trial data will provide estimates of
delivery costs, and an appropriate health related quality of life measure to analyse cost utility. This will support
preparation for a future full-scale economic evaluation of BCP.
On completion of my PhD I will apply for funding from Parkinson’s UK to develop training resources into a manual
and e-learning for SLTs to deliver BCP. I will also apply for a NIHR Advanced Fellowship award or Research for
Patient Benefit Grant to further explore questions such as: who is able to deliver the intervention; can self-
management be promoted using technology; how many sessions and what follow up is required to maintain gains.
I consequently aim to overcome barriers to implementation of BCP in the NHS, such as reduced SLT resources or
staffing. This further research directly addresses the concerns of key stakeholders and supports my journey
towards becoming an independent practitioner academic.
Project Management
My research will be governed by the UCL Graduate Research Degrees Code of Practice. I will be accountable to
my supervisory team, meeting every 2-4 weeks. Meetings will evaluate progress against fellowship milestones
recorded as part of the Doctoral School Research Log, and consider practical research elements such as
recruitment, research conduct and managing governance approvals.
I will meet at least twice yearly with collaborators and a thesis committee, who will provide access to broader
expertise and monitor my skills training programme. I will provide a full update at least annually to the PPI network
to demonstrate accountability.
Financial management will be through UCL, who have an established track record of managing larger grants and
individual salaries. Local financial management will be shared between myself, the Departmental Finance &
Research Team Leader and the primary supervisor.
Ethics
I will seek approval from the UCL Research Ethics Committee for WP1, and from the NHS Research Ethics
Committee and Health Research Authority for WP3. The UCL Joint Research Office will support completion of this
process.
The following ethical issues are anticipated:
1. Potential patient Distress: plwP can experience symptoms such as anxiety and depression, potentially
exacerbated by communication difficulties. The lead applicant and SLTs delivering BCP will be registered
members of the Health and Care Professions Council and Royal College of SLTs, and be trained to a high
level of communication and counselling skills. Participants will have access to their NHS Trusts’ specialist
Parkinson’s support and Patient Advice and Liaison services.
2. Information and consent: information and consent forms will be co-produced with plwP and follow
guidelines for accessibility. As per standard practice, recruiting SLTs will adapt information and support the
process of consent according to the Mental Capacity Act (2005). It is expected that participants will be able
to give informed consent with sufficient time and support from SLTs. Should impairment increase, so that
participants are no longer able to provide consent, the family carer will be consulted and initial discussions
used as an indication of the person’s wishes. Should a participant become uncomfortable with the
interventions they will be discontinued. The primary supervisor has 25 years’ experience of providing
accessible information about research for people with communication difficulties.
3. Video recording: I have experience of using video recording for BCP-Telehealth research and am familiar
with the policies and practices for consent and confidentiality within the relevant institutions. The primary
supervisor has 25 years’ experience of conducting research using video recording of patients with
communication difficulties.
4. Patient Data: I am familiar with the policies and practices for confidentiality, research governance and
Caldicott principles at UCL. Data will be stored on the highly secure UCL Data Safe Haven or encrypted
hard drives. Policies and procedures for storage and transfer of data will be followed for each NHS site.
Paper-based material will be kept in locked storage. The project will comply with the Data Protection Act
2018.
Success Criteria
If the following criteria are met, I will apply for an NIHR Advanced Fellowship award to fund a BCP effectiveness
trial.
1. Acceptability and feasibility of adapted BCP intervention: explored using reflexive thematic analysis of
participant/ SLT feedback forms and interviews, statistical analysis of adherence data, and data on cost of
delivery. PPI with a diverse range of stakeholders will be critical to minimise potential barriers to
implementation and ensure BCP meets the needs of plwP with varied communication and cognitive
difficulties.
2. Successful recruitment: slow recruitment has been an issue in Better Conversations and SLT trials with
plwP (45,46). The risk of insufficient recruitment will be mitigated through PPI maximising accessibility of
the study, and through close liaison with NHS sites. Face-to-face delivery is being investigated following
feedback that telehealth is a barrier to BCP-Telehealth recruitment. I will use the SEAR framework (58) at
each recruiting site to understand the recruitment process and improve it through additional support to
recruiting SLTs as required. Recruitment and retention data will be collected to predict rates for a future
trial.
3. Inclusion of under-served populations: Current Parkinson’s research largely concerns a cohort of
participants that does not resemble the diversity of plwP (43). I have used the NIHR Research Design
Service’s Equality Diversity and Inclusion toolkit to address this issue. For WP3, I aim to recruit over 10%
from minority ethnic groups to more closely reflect the UK population, at least 5% with complex
Parkinson’s and 10% with a cognitive impairment, given people with complex Parkinson’s make up a
significant number of speech and language therapy referrals (10) and to reflect the high incidence of
cognitive difficulties (59). Collaborating Trusts’ caseloads include under-served groups. I will seek advice
from support groups for plwP from ethnic minorities and Parkinson’s UK Race Equality in Research
Steering Group, and purposively select PPI members from under-served groups. Additional
communication methods, such as short videos from members of communities, may be required to support
recruitment. I will access training, supervision from Dr Gita Ramdharry who is leading work at UCL to
develop strategies to increase diversity in participant recruitment, and mentoring from Dr Victoria
Shepherd (Senior Research Fellow, Centre for Trials Research at Cardiff University) who specialises in
research involving under-served populations, in particular where informed consent can be challenging.
APPLICATION DETAILS.Patient & Public Involvement:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
PPI is central to BCP research (figure 1), which to date has been conducted in collaboration with key stakeholders
(plwP, family members, expert SLTs). I have facilitated steering and co-production groups responsible for:
overseeing the management of the project, informing recruitment and ethical procedures, generating a plan for
dissemination and future research and co-producing BCP-Telehealth therapy. Co-production involved equal power
in decision making between plwP, family members, SLTs and researchers, including decisions on: written and
video resources; therapy aims, structure and activities; and processes such as goal setting and monitoring
change.
I successfully secured a UCL Public Engagement grant to identify research priorities for this proposal. This
involved:
 advisory groups meeting online to identify initial research priorities
 use of social media to gather broad perspectives from plwP, researchers and SLTs on research priorities
 meeting with expert SLTs from five NHS Trusts, including those who work with people from a range of
ethnic origins and SLTs at a Parkinson’s Centre of Excellence, to explore current barriers to implementing
BCP and what our next steps in research should be
 working with an engagement group of plwP to synthesise this information and use consensus methods to
identify key research questions
The research aims, objectives and proposal for this study arose directly from the research priorities identified by
the engagement group. Group members have also co-authored the plain English summary of this application.
I have co-authored a paper evaluating BCP PPI processes based on survey responses from PPI group members,
documentation, outputs, and reflection using the Public Involvement Impact Assessment Framework. This
evaluation has enabled me to identify areas for development which are implemented in this proposal, for example:
having one core PPI group to allow for a stronger PPI voice; accounting for sufficient time and funding to explore
issues in depth; running face-to-face and online PPI meetings with different group sizes depending on participant
need; visiting support groups (e.g. The Jewish Care Disability Support and Social group, and Hema’s café) for
people from different ethnic origins; and purposively seeking people with more severe communication and
cognitive deficits to improve inclusion of under-served groups.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
PlwP, family members or friends and expert SLTs will be involved as equal partners throughout the co-design, co-
production and co-evaluation of this project (figure 1). I have expertise in co-production and an established PPI
network, with members who would like to continue working on the BCP project. I will collaborate with a PPI
network of 10 people to:
 co-design the research process (e.g. recruitment procedures and information for potential participants)
 co-investigate and decide upon outcome measures (WP1)
 update the BCP intervention (WP2)
 co-evaluate findings from WP3
 support dissemination (e.g. writing accessible summaries and jointly presenting findings)
I have used the NIHR Research Design Service’s Equality Diversity and Inclusion (EDI) toolkit to reflect on how to
improve EDI in this research. PPI forms a key part of aiming to minimise inequalities in outcomes, experience and
access as per the NHS 2022/2023 priorities. Based on feedback from previous PPI work and evaluation, I plan to:
recruit PPI members from support groups targeted at plwP from under-served groups

 collaborate with Parkinson’s UK Race Equality in Research Steering group to include people from cultural
backgrounds who have been previously less consulted in Parkinson’s research
 selectively recruit from rehabilitation centres to include PPI members with more severe communication
and cognitive difficulties
 use a flexible approach, with specific methods guided by the needs of participants to ensure that people
with diverse needs are able to meaningfully engage. For example, I will travel for one-to-one face-to-face
interviews to include plwP with more advanced symptoms, hold small (2-3 people) and large group
meetings, and offer online and in-person meetings.
All PPI members will be compensated at rates recommended by NIHR guidance. I have budgeted for 4-6 meetings
per year to allow significant time for PPI involvement, and someone with Parkinson’s will act as a joint facilitator.
Current PPI members made these recommendations as ways to increase the strength of their voice. Training will
be provided to empower PPI members to take equal power in decision making.
Public engagement will be evaluated in line with the Public Involvement Impact Assessment Framework and
recognised standards of good practice.

2026). One of these UCL students is a collaboration with the Dementia Research Centre. Dr Volkmer is also a
tertiary supervisor for a PhD student at the University of Oslo, Norway and has supervised more than 30
MSc/MRes research projects.
The proposed project aligns closely with Dr Volkmer’s expertise in intervention development and trials. Her recent
research has focused on the coproduction of communication partner training interventions for people with
language-led dementias (Better Conversations with Primary Progressive Aphasia; doi.org/10.1186/s12877-021-
02561-8) and piloting this within NHS settings (doi.org/10.1186/s40814-023-01301-6; doi.org/10/1111/bjhp.12690).
Dr Volkmer has extensive experience in mixed methods and qualitative research methods
(doi.org/10.1080/09638288.2022.2051080; doi.org/10.1177/16094069231171096).
Dr Volkmer has been successful in applying for a series of grants and fellowships including an NIHR Doctoral
Research Fellowship (2015-2019), Development Skills Enhancement Award (2020-2021) and her current NIHR
Advanced Fellowship (2022-ongoing). She has also secured other small grants from Alzheimer's Research UK,
UCL and UCLH charities.
Dr Volkmer has worked as a speech and language therapist (SLT) since 2002 and is a successful clinical
academic. She holds an honorary Consultant SLT post in the Cognitive Disorders Clinic at the National Hospital
for Neurology and Neurosurgery and leads a national speech and language therapy service for people with
dementia.
ADDITIONAL ACADEMIC SUPERVISOR 2
Name Dr Jennifer Foley
Institution University College London Hospitals
Position Senior Clinical Neuropsychologist and Honorary Lecturer
Description of support provided
Dr Foley is a clinical academic. At the National Hospital of Neurology and Neurosurgery, she leads the Parkinson’s
neuropsychology service. She is Honorary Lecturer at UCL Queen Square Institute of Neurology, leading a team
of researchers investigating how best to understand, assess and treat the complex cognitive and psychological
symptoms of Parkinson’s. She has received £150K in research funding from Parkinson’s UK, has 56 publications
(37 first/last author; 1223 citations), and has been commissioned to write evidence-based clinical guidelines on
Parkinson’s for the British Psychological Society and American Psychological Association. Her expertise will
support the development of a refined BCP intervention that meets the needs of plwP with cognitive impairments
and their communication partners.
With extensive experience of using public engagement to improve translation of clinical research and influence
healthcare policy, Dr Foley will ensure that the project meets the needs of key stakeholders and has meaningful
impact. She has received £15k from UCL to use co-creative approaches to improve understanding of the unmet
needs in Parkinson’s, and has worked with community groups to create patient-led podcasts and videos on mental
health in Parkinson’s. BCP is a holistic approach that aims to include plwP with a range of symptoms, such as
mental health difficulties. Dr Foley leads Parkinson’s UK Excellence Network for Mental Health, a professional
network dedicated to improving mental health services for people with Parkinson’s, and has worked with patient
charities to advise parliament and governmental departments on the psychological needs of people with
Parkinson’s.

Description of support provided
Dr Gita Ramdharry is a physiotherapist working as a Consultant Allied Health Professional (AHP) at the National
Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery (NHNN) and an Honorary Associate Professor at the UCL Institute of
Neurology. She works as a Clinical Academic in the NHS conducting clinical research and was the first AHP to
receive NIHR BRC funding support at NHNN. She is clinical AHP lead for the Centre for Neuromuscular Diseases
working at consultant level. She also leads the Neuromuscular Rehabilitation Research Group, is a Principal
Investigator and Equality, Diversity and Inclusion lead at the Department of Neuromuscular Diseases, UCL. Dr
Ramdharry sits on the British Myology Society council and has developed AHP focused study days and training in
neurological diseases. Her clinical and research interests focus on rehabilitation interventions for people with rare
neurological diseases, ranging from impairment-focused treatment to self-management and behaviour change.
Through NIHR, NIH, charity and overseas grants, she has been awarded £2.6 million as a PI or co-applicant. She
has authored 67 peer reviewed journal articles and five book chapters. She currently supervises 6 PhD students (3
as primary supervisor), supervising two PhD students to completion.
Dr Ramdharry will provide key input through her clinical expertise in the management of people with neurological
diseases but also by helping me navigate the route to consultant level practice and clinical academia during and
beyond this fellowship.
PRACTICE SUPERVISOR 2
Name Dr Alan Whone
Institution North Bristol NHS Trust
Position Honorary Consultant Neurologist
Description of support provided
Dr Alan Whone is Consultant Senior Lecturer in Movement Disorders at University of Bristol and Honorary
Consultant Neurologist at North Bristol Trust. Dr Whone leads a clinical service at the Bristol Brain Centre, that
conducts approximately 4000 Parkinson’s visits per year from the diagnostic to the palliative stage.
Dr Whone additionally leads a programme of research in Parkinson’s and since 2010 has been Principal Applicant
or Co-applicant on £5-million of peer reviewed grant funding (Parkinson’s UK, NIHR) and £1 million of industry or
local charities research funding. With significant clinical, research and lecturing experience, Dr Whone is ideally
placed to support development towards my aims of furthering my knowledge and experience in the
pathophysiology, aetiology, assessment and management of Parkinson’s. I will meet monthly with Dr Whone to
oversee my professional development.
Dr Whone has an international collaborative research network and has given multiple invited research lectures in
the UK and internationally. He is co-director of The Parkinson’s and Other Movement Disorders Health Integration
Team, a team of clinical staff, scientists, patients and other representatives, established to improve treatment and
support for people with Parkinson’s within Bristol and beyond. Through these varied networks, Dr Whone will
provide opportunities for collaboration and dissemination outside of my own and University College London’s
networks.
With experience of leading a regional Parkinson’s clinical service, Dr Whone will provide valuable leadership
support towards the long-term aim of implementing Better Conversations with Parkinson’s within the NHS.
Collaborations
Bristol Speech and Language Therapy Research Unit (BSLTRU) has an eminent history of carrying out
research in the field of speech and language therapy and supporting clinical researchers. BSLTRU will provide a
work base for the duration of the fellowship with access to the mentorship, support and knowledge of experienced
clinical researchers based there. For example, Dr Katherine Broomfield’s expertise in patient experience and co-
production will support PPI work throughout the project. BSLTRU is hosted by North Bristol NHS Trust (NBT),
the lead site for this research project. NBT’s Research and Innovation department will support each stage of the
research, including design, ethical approval and dissemination.
NBT is host to Bristol Brain Centre, the first centre of its kind in the country to bring together research teams in
the areas of multiple sclerosis, dementia and movement disorders such as Parkinson’s. Dr Alan Whone
(Consultant neurologist) has agreed to support this research, with access to mentoring, support with PPI and trial
recruitment, and opportunities for professional development.
In order to support implementation of BCP and gain in-depth understanding of leading clinical practice in
Parkinson’s, I intend to visit the two Parkinson’s Centres of Excellence in the UK (Derby and King’s College
London). I have established connections with speech and language therapists at these centres, with a view to
learning about care pathways and therapy provision, and building clinical networks. I will attend multidisciplinary
clinics to further my knowledge of the management of Parkinson’s and related neurological disorders.
I will also build research networks through collaboration with Dr Victoria Shepherd at Cardiff University, and
through participation in the UCL Better Conversations Lab. Dr Victoria Shepherd has agreed to support with the
recruitment to work package 3, and has expertise in research amongst populations where informed consent can
be challenging. I am a current member of the UCL Better Conversations Lab, led by Dr Suzanne Beeke. This is a
team of academics and clinical academics specialising in the scientific analysis of the impact of communication
difficulties and needs on everyday conversations, and the development and evaluation of communication partner
training interventions. I will connect with the Lab’s network of clinicians through involvement with ventures such as
the biennial international Better Conversations conference and training courses for SLTs.
To build my understanding of international research in my specialist area, and develop important relationships for
future collaborations, I will visit the Universities of Colorado Boulder and Washington. The Human Interaction
Lab (University of Colorado Boulder) is a specialist research lab aiming to improve communication for people with
neurological speech disorders. The lab hosts researchers who specialise in dysarthric speech, listener responses
to dysarthric speech, and communicative participation. I have provisional agreement from Dr Carolyn Baylor to
visit the Department of Rehabilitation Medicine (University of Washington), which is a multidisciplinary faculty of
clinicians and researchers, and national leader in rehabilitation research and training. Dr Carolyn Baylor has
expertise on issues related to life participation outcomes for adults with acquired communication disorders, with
current funded projects related to the development of novel patient-reported outcomes measures and life-
participation and intervention resources to help adults with Parkinson’s disease stay connected and engaged with
their life activities. As with BCP research, this work focuses on supporting family members and friends alongside
the people with Parkinson’s.
Finally, I have established networks at Parkinson’s UK, the leading charity for people living with Parkinson’s in
the UK. Parkinson’s UK have funded BCP-Telehealth research and have provisionally agreed to support
recruitment to PPI groups and WP1, and support with EDI issues through collaboration with members of their
Race and Equality in Research steering group.
Host Organisation Support Statement
The following statement of support should be composed jointly by the Heads of Department / Senior Managers of
the employing host organisation and the partner organisation.
The Heads of Department / Senior Managers of these organisations will be able to add the statement of support to
the form once they have confirmed participation. Invitations to participate are sent by the applicant via the
'Participants and Signatories' section of the form.
Statement of support
Host organisation
The applicant will be based in the Department of Language and Cognition, in UCL’s Faculty of Brain Sciences
(ranked eighth in the 2023 QS World University Rankings). Her proposed research programme represents an
excellent fit with the research profile and strategic aims of the Department; an environment in which Philippa will
be supported to thrive as a clinical academic.
The Faculty was first in the UK for research power in the Psychology, Psychiatry and Neuroscience panel in
REF2021. The department hosts an HCPC-accredited speech and language therapy programme, and has
interdisciplinary links to other clinical education programmes. There is also easy access to the RDS, Clinical Trials
Units and a Joint Research Office.
The host Department is an international centre of excellence in speech, language and communication disorders
research. Staff hold awards from a range of funders including NIHR, MRC, ESRC and third sector funders such as
the Alzheimer’s Society and Parkinson’s UK. The department currently hosts four pre-doctoral clinical and
practitioner academic fellowship (PCAF) awardees, one pre-doctoral local authority fellowship awardee, and
another 16 completed PCAF awardees. Two current students are NIHR doctoral funded. Three NIHR funded
students have recently completed PhDs, with one awarded a Development and Skills Enhancement Award (Oct
2020-Dec 2021), and more recently an Advanced Fellowship (July 2022-June 2027).
Philippa will join a vibrant community of doctoral and post-doctoral researchers. Our doctoral community currently
numbers 25 continuing plus 8 new students. We offer a structured programme of support for early career
researchers, including modules relevant to Philippa’s research (e.g. Statistics and Research Methods) and
important skills such as ‘Getting Published’ and ‘Postdoctoral Funding Landscape’. Doctoral students receive
training in research integrity and ethical conduct, and are co-located in a large ‘lab’ office, with technical and
finance staff available to support IT and all aspects of award management. Philippa will engage with the numerous
development opportunities arranged by the Department. The Early Careers Researcher Group provides
opportunities to obtain insights from established researchers on relevant development areas and the annual post-
graduate student conference provides opportunities to practice presenting research findings to wider audiences.
The department strongly supports a multi-stage development pathway for clinical researchers, and we will use
these experiences to support Philippa’s future research career. Philippa’s proposal identifies a clear pathway to
develop the outputs/resources and empirical research produced by this fellowship. The Department is ideally
placed to support this development. It hosts experts in the production and evaluation of complex interventions for
communication impairments and has a track record in releasing interventions for use in clinical practice. Most
significantly the department hosts the Better Conversations lab: an international group of clinical-researchers
specialising in the development and implementation of conversation-based interventions. Experience across this
pathway will facilitate Philippa to transition to the next phase of this research, with potential impacts across a wide
range of clinical populations. The group has particular expertise in PPI and co-production, a central feature of
Philippa’s proposed research programme.
The applicant’s primary supervisor, Professor Steven Bloch, is a leading expert in conversation analysis and its
application to progressive degenerative conditions. He has a track record in developing new interventions for
people with motor speech disorders and has received research funding from a range of funders including the
NIHR. Alongside the supervisory team, Philippa will be supported by a thesis committee, meeting with her twice
yearly and drawing together researchers with relevant expertise to review and provide feedback on her research
plans and progress. This aims to enhance the theoretical and methodological quality of the research and identify
areas for further support. In addition, a post-graduate tutor oversees all doctoral students.
The Department and Faculty are committed to EDI and recognise that EDI is critical to progression. The Faculty’s
Gender Equity Lead and Disability Equity Lead work across the Faculty to promote best practice and support EDI.
The division holds a Silver Athena Swan award and is aspiring to Gold status in our next submission. We have
made substantial progress in diversifying our student and staff profile over the last 5 years and we actively monitor
issues such as potential awarding gaps.
NHS Partner Organisation
The Speech and Language Therapy team within the Therapy and Rehabilitation Department at the National
Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery (NHNN) and the UCL Division of Psychology and Language Sciences
have a well-established partnership, providing an excellent environment for Philippa to build a strong practitioner
academic career. If the award is made, Philippa will be integrated into both departments enabling her to conduct
clinical development activity appropriate to the Doctoral Clinical and Practitioner Academic Fellowship (DCAF).
The Department has an excellent track record in supporting Allied Health Professionals (AHPs) to pursue clinical
academic careers. Significant numbers of AHPs pursue clinical-academic training, including NIHR internships,
PCAF awards and clinical doctorates. Philippa will be supervised by a multidisciplinary team from UCL and NHNN,
ideally suited to draw together the theoretical foundations, methodological approaches and clinical expertise
required to deliver this research programme and develop Philippa’s career as a practitioner academic.
The department is committed to achieving true equity and diversity in its staff, who are not only included but feel a
sense of belonging in their workplace. The department is clear that when this is achieved, it best serves its diverse
community as cultural sensitivity will be ingrained, with a shared vision to provide equity in access to services. The
department is working with the BAME and LGBTQIA+ networks at NHNN and the wider UCLH trust on
implementing EDI strategies.
As Philippa starts her DCAF, she will work the Cognitive Disorders Clinic and Movement Disorders services, where
she will work with world leading neurologists and clinical academic speech and language therapists developing her
skills in the assessment and management of people with Parkinson's and dementias. The Department has
extensive experience in supporting clinical academic fellows to ensure clinical and research time are well
balanced. We will ensure Philippa's role is integrated within the clinical service and help her balance clinical and
research needs throughout the fellowship.

Description: PPI member fees
Type of cost: HEI
Please describe fully: PPI member fees
Justification of Cost: PPI is a central part of this project, to ensure that the research and resulting intervention is
relevant and acceptable to stakeholders. PPI input will be throughout the entire project (see Figure 1), with a
particular focus on co-investigating work package 1 and co-producing work-package 2. Co-production is
recognised as essential when designing interventions for health and social care services in frameworks and
guidance for developing complex interventions. PPI members will be reimbursed at rates recommended by NIHR
guidance (https://www.nihr.ac.uk/documents/payment-guidance-for-researchers-and-professionals/27392). I have
budgeted for 10 participants (plwP or family members or friends) to participate for the equivalent of bi-monthly 2-
hour meetings, or half a day quarterly over the course of the study (£50 x 30 meetings x 10 = £15,000). I have
followed feedback from current PPI group members in allocating more time to PPI to allow time for full exploration
of ideas and to strengthen the voice of stakeholders. Extensive time for meaningful involvement is required given
the time taken to make topics accessible to people with complex communication and cognitive needs. Work
package 2 will involve more regular meetings (4 additional half day meetings) to co-produce the BCP intervention.
Investment in stakeholders’ time is key to ensure that the intervention meets the needs of plwP, family members
and SLTs, therefore maximising acceptability and the likelihood of successful future implementation in the NHS.
The cost of this additional time is £3000.

with the lead applicant.
